topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a shimmering expanse of silk and forced merriment. I clutched my champagne flute like a lifeline, the crystal cool against my palm. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. He was laughing, his head tilted back in that easy way I used to recognize, and the sight hit me like a physical blow. My breath hitched, a shallow, tight thing in my chest. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. I took a deliberate step backward, bumping into a passing waiter, and the sudden jarring contact made me flinch visibly. I needed air, needed distance, but my feet felt rooted to the marble floor. He spotted me, and for a split second, the music seemed to fade, leaving only the roaring in my own ears. I managed a strained, small smile, a performance I instantly regretted, and turned sharply toward the periphery of the room, searching desperately for an exit.

[story 2]

I was trying to appear engaged in a conversation about the terrible catering when I saw her standing near the floral arrangements. Sarah. She looked stunning, radiant in emerald green, and the way she moved, graceful and unconcerned, was a sharp contrast to the knot tightening in my stomach. My hands began to sweat, the fine moisture prickling my palms. I forced myself to keep walking, pretending to be interested in the hors d'oeuvres, but my gaze kept snagging on her. When our eyes met—for just a second—a strange, painful recognition flooded my system. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to simply disappear. I gripped the strap of my clutch so tightly my knuckles turned white. I mumbled an excuse to a passing acquaintance and practically fled toward the terrace doors, needing the cool night air to wash away the sudden, intense heat rising in my cheeks.

[story 3]

The noise level was deafening, a wall of celebratory chatter and booming music. I was positioned near the edge of the patio, nursing a lukewarm drink, trying to observe the scene from a safe remove. Then, there he was, standing near the entrance, talking animatedly with someone. It was him. The sound of his voice, familiar and warm, cut through the din, and a sudden, sharp ache settled low in my abdomen. My vision seemed to narrow, focusing solely on him. I watched as he turned, and for a fleeting moment, our paths converged. Time stretched thin. I felt a distinct tremor run through my legs, making it hard to remain steady. I quickly looked down at my shoes, focusing intently on the pattern of the pavement, praying that he wouldn't see the raw, exposed feeling I was trying so hard to conceal.
